## Flat vs. relational data tables

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Flat file**

One giant table - everything repeated on every row

|date | site | lat | lon | species | count|
|--|--|--|--|--|--|
|2024-01 | A | 11 | -74 | fish1 | 3|
|2024-01 | A | 11 | -74 | fish2 | 1|
|2024-01 | A | 11 | -74 | fish3 | 7|

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Relational**

Separate, linked tables — shared info stored *once*

```
site.csv:      site, date, lat, lon
species.csv:   site, species, count
```

🔗 Linked by a shared identifier 
    (an **ID** column)
:::
::::



::: {.notes}
This is the concept underlying Darwin Core Archives: a "core" table (e.g. Event) and one or more "extension" tables (e.g. Occurrence, DNA extension) linked by eventID/occurrenceID. It reduces repetition and errors — fix the date once in the Event table instead of on every one of a thousand species rows. Keep this fast for people who already know relational databases; it's here mainly to set up tomorrow's DNA data session where we'll have Event → Occurrence → DNA extension as three linked tables.
:::
